title_en: Al-Uqubat — Criminal Law and Prescribed Penalties
content_en:
The criminal law section of Al-Fiqh al-Islami wa-Adillatuhu addresses one of the most discussed and often misunderstood areas of Islamic jurisprudence. Dr. al-Zuhayli presents Islamic criminal law within its proper conceptual framework: a system of justice rooted in the protection of five universal necessities (daruriyyat al-khams) — life, intellect, lineage, property, and religion — around which all Shari'ah rulings can be understood.

The book distinguishes between three categories of punishment in Islamic law: hudud (fixed penalties for offenses whose punishment is explicitly specified in the texts), qisas (equal retaliation for bodily harm and homicide), and ta'zir (discretionary penalties left to the judge for offenses without a fixed punishment). This three-part taxonomy is essential for understanding Islamic criminal law, which is far more complex and nuanced than popular discussions suggest.

On hudud, the author covers the six offenses with fixed penalties: zina (unlawful intercourse), qadhf (false accusation of zina), theft, consumption of alcohol, hirabah (armed robbery), and apostasy. For each offense, he presents the conditions that must be met before the hadd may be applied — conditions so stringent (four witnesses observing the act of penetration for zina; the stolen amount meeting the nisab and being taken from a secure place for theft) that scholars have noted the primary deterrent function of hudud over their punitive application. Dr. al-Zuhayli presents the significant scholarly disagreement on apostasy and the conditions under which it applies.

Qisas (retaliation) is presented as a system of legal equality before the law: the principle that the penalty for unlawful killing or wounding is equivalent harm to the perpetrator, but that the heirs of the victim may pardon or accept blood money (diya) instead. The humanitarian dimension of qisas — which gives families the right to forgive — is highlighted as evidence of the system's profound ethical foundation. Ta'zir covers the broad discretionary space available to Islamic courts for deterring contemporary offenses not covered by the textual penalties.
